verdicts.name: Obvious lottery / prize winner scam
verdicts.reasoning: This is a classic advance-fee/lottery scam: it claims the recipient won a large cash prize via a random email selection, requests extensive PII, and demands a gift card 'processing fee' to release winnings — none of which is consistent with any legitimate Google promotion. The sender domain 'google-promotions-intl.com' is not affiliated with Google.
indicators: Spoofed/lookalike domain 'google-promotions-intl.com' impersonating Google
Unsolicited lottery win notification with no prior participation
Requests sensitive PII (full name, address, DOB, phone, occupation)
Demands a $150 gift card payment as a 'processing fee' — classic advance-fee fraud
Generic greeting 'Dear Lucky Winner'
Artificial urgency: 7-day deadline or prize forfeited
Implausibly large prize ($450,000) awarded via random email selection
Fake reference code and invented job title to appear official

verdicts.name: Obvious bank impersonation with spoofed domain
verdicts.reasoning: The sender domain 'chasebank-online.com' is not Chase's legitimate domain (chase.com), and the dispute link points to 'chase-secure-alerts.com', another lookalike domain unaffiliated with Chase. The message uses classic phishing tactics including urgency ('within 12 hours'), a fabricated suspicious transaction, and a redacted phone number to pressure victims into clicking a fraudulent link.
indicators: Sender domain 'chasebank-online.com' spoofs Chase but is not the legitimate chase.com
Dispute link goes to 'chase-secure-alerts.com', a lookalike domain unrelated to Chase
Artificial 12-hour deadline to create urgency
Redacted/placeholder phone number '1-800-XXX-XXXX' typical of phishing templates
Generic greeting 'Dear Chase Customer' rather than the recipient's actual name
Foreign transaction detail (Lagos, NG) used as a fear trigger

verdicts.name: Obvious package delivery scam with redelivery fee
verdicts.reasoning: The sender domain 'fed-ex-delivery.com' is not an official FedEx domain, and the redelivery link points to 'fedex-redelivery.track-pkg.com' — a lookalike domain unrelated to fedex.com. The request for a small fee combined with fake urgency and spoofed branding is a classic parcel delivery phishing tactic.
indicators: Sender domain 'fed-ex-delivery.com' is not the official fedex.com
Redelivery link uses a lookalike domain 'track-pkg.com' rather than fedex.com
Request for payment of $2.99 redelivery fee — common credential/payment harvesting lure
Urgency tactic: 5-day deadline before package is returned
Encoding artifact in subject line ('â€

verdicts.name: Obvious Microsoft / Apple credential phish
verdicts.reasoning: The sender domain 'micros0ft-account.com' and the link domain 'micros0ft-secure.com' both use a zero ('0') substituted for the letter 'o' to spoof Microsoft, which is a classic lookalike domain technique. Combined with urgency tactics (24-hour lockout threat), a vague 'Dear Customer' greeting, and a suspicious third-party verification URL, this is a clear phishing attempt.
indicators: Spoofed sender domain using '0' instead of 'o': micros0ft-account.com
Lookalike link domain: account-verify.micros0ft-secure.com
Generic greeting 'Dear Customer' instead of the recipient's name
Artificial urgency: 24-hour account lockout threat
Unrecognized geographic location (Moscow, Russia) used to induce panic
Verification URL does not point to any official microsoft.com domain

verdicts.name: Obvious IRS tax refund scam
verdicts.reasoning: This email impersonates the IRS using a spoofed domain (irs-taxrefund-portal.com) and directs the recipient to a fraudulent lookalike URL (taxportal-gov.com) to harvest banking and personal information, which is a classic credential/data phishing attack. The IRS never initiates contact via email to request banking details or personal verification through a web portal.
indicators: Sender domain 'irs-taxrefund-portal.com' is not the official IRS domain (irs.gov)
Link destination 'irs-refund-claim.taxportal-gov.com' is a lookalike domain, not irs.gov
Request to submit direct deposit/banking information via an external portal
Artificial urgency: 10-day deadline with threat of refund forfeiture
Generic greeting 'Dear Taxpayer' instead of addressing recipient by name
Fabricated Case ID to add false legitimacy
The IRS does not initiate contact by email regarding refunds or banking updates

verdicts.name: Obvious tech support / 'your computer is infected' scam

indicators: Sender domain 'defender-secure-support.com' is not affiliated with Microsoft
Urgent scare tactics listing fake malware detections to pressure the recipient
Request to call a third-party phone number rather than directing to official Microsoft support
Artificial 24-hour deadline threatening permanent file encryption
Fake 'Case ID' to add false legitimacy
Contradictory disclaimer ('Microsoft will never ask for your password') used to appear trustworthy while still running a scam

verdicts.name: Spear-phishing: CEO impersonation requesting an urgent wire
verdicts.reasoning: This is a classic Business Email Compromise (BEC) / CEO fraud attempt: a purported executive ('David Chen, CEO') is sending from a personal Gmail account rather than a corporate domain, creating artificial urgency, claiming to be unavailable (in a meeting), and requesting an immediate large wire transfer to an unknown vendor with full banking details provided. These are textbook hallmarks of BEC fraud.
indicators: Sender uses a personal Gmail address (david.chen.priv@gmail.com) instead of a corporate Meridian Manufacturing domain
High-urgency language: 'Urgent', 'before end of day', 'process immediately'
CEO claiming to be unavailable to speak, preventing verbal verification
Large wire transfer ($38,500) to an unknown overseas vendor
Full bank account and routing details pre-supplied to streamline fraudulent transfer
Vague promise to 'explain details later' to discourage questions
Generic 'Sent from my iPhone' footer used to excuse brevity and lack of verification

verdicts.name: Spear-phishing: CFO impersonation requesting confidential financial info
verdicts.reasoning: This email exhibits classic business email compromise (BEC) hallmarks: a plausible authority figure (Rebecca) using an off-channel personal email (protonmail), a compelling out-of-office pretext (daughter's recital, laptop left at work), urgency (board call tomorrow), and a request to exfiltrate sensitive internal financial documents (Q1 P&L, revenue-by-segment data) to an unverified external address.
indicators: Sender uses a personal ProtonMail address rather than a corporate email, claiming to be a finance executive
Off-channel communication justified by a sympathetic personal excuse (daughter's recital, forgot laptop)
Urgent deadline (board call first thing tomorrow) designed to pressure quick action without verification
Requests sensitive, confidential internal financial data (Q1 close summary, P&L slide, revenue-by-segment) be sent externally
Specific internal details (Marcus, Q1 close, last Thursday) included to add credibility — typical of BEC reconnaissance
'Sent from my phone' footer used to excuse informal channel and deter scrutiny

verdicts.name: Sophisticated shared-document trap referencing a real colleague
verdicts.reasoning: The sender domain 'google-docs-share.com' and the link domain 'google-drive-sharing.com' are both lookalike domains impersonating Google, not legitimate Google infrastructure (which uses google.com). This is a classic phishing technique designed to harvest Google account credentials when the victim 'signs in' to view the document.
indicators: Sender domain 'google-docs-share.com' is not an official Google domain
Link destination 'google-drive-sharing.com' is a lookalike/spoofed domain, not docs.google.com
Prompt to sign in with Google account credentials to view the document — credential harvesting vector
Fabricated urgency around a 'CONFIDENTIAL' budget document and upcoming meeting to entice the recipient to click

verdicts.name: Obvious crypto giveaway / doubling scam
verdicts.reasoning: This is a classic cryptocurrency doubling scam: it impersonates the Ethereum Foundation using a lookalike domain (eth-foundation-giveaway.net) and promises to double any ETH sent to a wallet address, which is a well-known fraud pattern designed to steal cryptocurrency. The sender domain has no affiliation with the real Ethereum Foundation (ethereum.org), and the 'send crypto to receive double back' scheme is a textbook crypto phishing/scam technique.
indicators: Lookalike/spoofed domain 'eth-foundation-giveaway.net' impersonating the Ethereum Foundation
Classic cryptocurrency doubling scam (send X ETH, receive 2X back)
Fake urgency: 'ENDS TODAY', 'pool closes when it's empty'
Suspicious claim page URL on the same fraudulent domain
Unverified wallet address used to collect victim funds
Fabricated social proof ('3,800 community members have already participated')

verdicts.name: Obvious unclaimed-inheritance advance-fee scam
verdicts.reasoning: This is a classic advance-fee fraud (419 scam) posing as a legal inheritance notice, using fabricated urgency, a vague claim of shared surname, and a large sum of money to lure the recipient into providing personal information and eventually money. It exhibits all hallmark indicators of unsolicited scam mail rather than a targeted credential-phishing attack.
indicators: Generic greeting 'Dear Beneficiary' with no specific name
Unsolicited claim of a large inheritance (USD 8.4 million) from an unknown deceased client
Vague connection to recipient based solely on shared surname
Urgent pressure tactic: funds will be 'declared unclaimed by the authorities'
Request for personal information (full name, address, phone number) from a cold email
Self-created domain 'aldous-legal-chambers.org' with no verifiable third-party presence
Classic advance-fee fraud (419 scam) narrative structure

verdicts.name: Obvious student-loan forgiveness scam
verdicts.reasoning: This email is a classic phishing/scam attempt: it uses a lookalike domain (studentaid-relief-program.com) impersonating the U.S. Department of Education's official studentaid.gov, creates artificial urgency with a 72-hour deadline, requests highly sensitive PII (SSN and FSA ID), and demands an $89 'processing fee' — which is not a feature of any legitimate federal student loan forgiveness program.
indicators: Sender domain 'studentaid-relief-program.com' is not an official government (.gov) domain
Requests Social Security number and FSA ID credentials via a non-government website
Charges an $89 'enrollment processing fee' — legitimate federal programs never charge fees
Artificial 72-hour deadline to create urgency and pressure action
Vague claim that 'our records indicate you may qualify' with no specific account information
All-caps urgency language ('FINAL NOTICE', 'ACTION REQUIRED', 'INCOMPLETE')
No verifiable contact information or official agency branding beyond a fabricated program reference number

verdicts.name: Payroll-diversion via employee impersonation
verdicts.reasoning: This email exhibits the classic 'direct deposit redirect' social engineering attack: the sender uses a personal Gmail account claiming inability to access work systems, creates urgency around a payroll deadline, and attempts to get payroll staff to process a bank account change outside of normal secure channels. This is a well-documented pattern used to fraudulently redirect employee paychecks.
indicators: Sender uses a personal Gmail address (jasonpratt.payroll@gmail.com) rather than a corporate email to conduct a sensitive payroll/HR request
Claims inability to access work email or the HR portal — a common pretext to bypass secure verification processes
Urgency created by mentioning the next payroll run and a 'now-closed' old account to pressure quick action
Requests to send sensitive banking information (routing and account numbers) outside of the official secure portal
'@gmail.com' domain with 'payroll' in the address is suspicious and possibly designed to appear authoritative

verdicts.name: Managed-IT vendor impersonation requesting remote access
verdicts.reasoning: This email exhibits a classic phishing setup: it claims to be from an MSP performing an emergency patch, references a vague embargoed vulnerability to create urgency without providing verifiable details, and primes the recipient to expect a future 'verification step' — a common social engineering technique to harvest credentials or account info. The sender domain (northbridge-msp-support.com) uses a support-subdomain pattern often associated with spoofed or lookalike domains rather than an official company domain.
indicators: Sender domain 'northbridge-msp-support.com' is a hyphenated support-style domain that could be a lookalike/spoofed domain
Vague reference to a 'critical endpoint vulnerability' under embargo — no verifiable details provided
Pre-announcement of a future 'verification step' for accounts/machines — classic credential-harvesting setup
Urgency framing ('emergency rollout', 'this week') without actionable verification
No action requested now but primes recipient to comply with a follow-up request

verdicts.name: Law-firm legal-demand pretext for confidential records
verdicts.reasoning: This email uses classic social engineering tactics: it creates urgency around a legal 'preservation obligation,' instructs the recipient to keep it secret from colleagues ('not circulated internally'), and requests confirmation of identity/role — all without any verifiable case details or formal documentation. The instruction to handle the matter 'discreetly' is a hallmark of business email compromise and phishing attempts designed to isolate the target.
indicators: Instructs recipient to keep matter secret from colleagues ('not circulated internally beyond those with a need to know')
Requests confirmation of recipient's role and identity before providing any verifiable details
Vague legal threat ('preservation obligation has attached') with no supporting documentation or specific case details
Urgency framing ('expedited basis,' 'required timeframe') designed to pressure quick action
No verifiable firm website, bar association reference, or other legitimizing details provided
Sender domain 'whitlock-graves-legal.com' is not a well-known law firm domain and may be a lookalike

verdicts.name: Fake-recruiter onboarding harvesting PII
verdicts.reasoning: This is an unsolicited recruitment email with vague job details, unrealistic promises of a fast hiring process ('onboarded within a few days'), and a request for personal contact information (phone number), all classic hallmarks of job-scam spam. There is no verifiable company presence indicated, the domain 'brightpath-talenthub.com' appears generic and unverifiable, and the message uses high-pressure appeal with little concrete information about the role.
indicators: Unsolicited outreach with no prior contact or context
Vague job description with no company or role specifics
Unusually fast onboarding promise ('within a few days')
Request for personal phone number in reply
Generic, unverifiable recruiter domain (brightpath-talenthub.com)
Suspiciously broad pay range with no role details
